system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 1: Getting Started

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 1 — Getting Started

_Pages 6-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Open Position Chords requirement

> You need to know the basic open-position chords as well as the standard barre chord
> fingerings. Changing from one chord to another should not be a problem. If you don’t
> know these chords, learn them well before proceeding.

**Significance (p. 6).** This establishes the prerequisite technical baseline the entire series assumes, making it the entry gate for all subsequent instruction.

## Barre chords: sixth-string root

> With the root on the sixth string.
>
> MAJOR
> 1304211
>
> DOMINANT
> to 2 14

**Significance (p. 7).** This presents the two essential movable barre chord forms (major and dominant 7th) rooted on the sixth string, which are foundational shapes for all jazz chord work.

## Barre chords: fifth-string root

> With the root on the fifth string.
>
> MAJOR
> x1 33 3 X
>
> DOMINANT
> 41.3.4 4.4

**Significance (p. 7).** These fifth-string-root barre forms complete the movable chord vocabulary needed to cover the entire fretboard in major and dominant voicings.

## Standard notation: pitch and rhythm

> Reading standard music notation is a necessary skill for anyone interested in learning to
> play jazz. Once you get the idea, you'll find that it’s really easy, and then a whole world of
> instructional books and great music will open up for you. There are two basic elements to
> standard notation: pitch and rhythm. Every note has a particular note name (pitch) and
> particular duration (rhythm). The line or space on a note falls on tells you the pitch.

**Significance (p. 8).** This frames standard notation as a non-negotiable skill for jazz and defines its two fundamental dimensions, establishing why the book uses it throughout.

## Time signature definition

> Every piece has numbers at the beginning, called the time signature, that tell us how to
> count the time. The top number represents the number of beats or counts per measure.
> The bottom number represents the type of note receiving one count. The most common
> time signature, 4 is shown below. In 4 time, there are four beats per measure, and the
> quarter note (d) receives one beat.

**Significance (p. 8).** This defines the time signature concept and decodes the top/bottom number meanings, which are necessary for reading every notated example in the series.

## Note values and rests

> The appearance of the note—the type of note head or stem that it has—tells you the
> rhythm. Rests tell you when and how long not to play, which is also an important aspect of
> rhythm.

**Significance (p. 8).** This rule that rests are as important as notes frames silence as a rhythmic element, a key interpretive concept for jazz phrasing.

## Tablature definition

> Tablature is a system of notation that graphically repre- + te ¢
> sents the strings and frets of the guitar fingerboard. Each 5 be
> note is indicated by placing a number, which shows the fret Baah :
> to play, on the appropriate string.

**Significance (p. 8).** This defines tablature as the parallel notation system used alongside standard notation throughout the book, the primary reading tool for most guitar students.

## Progression / changes terminology

> You need to be familiar with these basic progressions. Progression refers to the movement
> of one chord to another. In jazz,a progression is often referred to as the changes.

**Significance (p. 9).** This introduces the jazz-specific term “changes” as a synonym for chord progression, vocabulary the student will encounter constantly in real jazz contexts.

## Strumming coordination requirement

> You will need to know some basic strumming patterns as well. The patterns themselves
> are not that important. The idea is that you should have the coordination to strum a
> pattern while changing chords. Read the section on “Basic Strumming Technique” on page
> 87 for some tips.

**Significance (p. 9).** This defines the minimum rhythmic-coordination prerequisite (maintaining a pattern while changing chords) and cross-references where technique tips live.

## Book A/B section architecture

> Each chapter is
> divided into lessons, and each lesson is divided into two separate sections. The “A”
> sections deal with harmonic and chordal topics and the “B” sections cover information
> about single line improvisation.

**Significance (p. 5).** This explains the dual-track architecture of every lesson, which governs how a student should navigate the entire curriculum.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
